subject: Chemistry
solution:
Van der Waal's Equation of state for one mole

P + a V 2 V - b = RT

At low pressure, the characteristic constant (a) becomes significant whereas (b) becomes negligible. So (b) is ignored.

P + a V 2 V = RT

PV + a V = RT

PV RT = RT RT - a VRT = 1 - a VRT

At low pressure for most gases z < 1.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The van der Waals' constant ′a′ is directly proportional to the intermolecular force of attraction; hence, the gas with greater value of  ′a′ can be most easily liquefied.The values of Vander Waal's constant 'a' for the given gases are:
    Gases                    Vander Waal's Constant 'a'
A. Ar                           1.35 L2 atm mol2
B. CH4                       2.25L2 atm  mol2
C. H2O                       5.536L2 atm mol2
D. C6H6                       18.57L2 atm mol2
∴ Increasing order of 'a' for the given gases
A<B<C<D

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Method 1:
The extent of adsorption is directly proportional to the critical temperature. As H2 has the lowest critical temperature, it will have the lowest extent of adsorption.

Method 2:
The value of adsorption is dependent on the intermolecular forces of attraction. Among the given options, H2 will have the weakest van der Waal's force (London Dispersion), in which only 2 electrons are involved.

Hence, as a force of attraction is the weakest for H2, it will have the lowest adsorption value.

subject: Chemistry
options: Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy more volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be lesser compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>

Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy lesser volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be lesser compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>

Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy more volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be more compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>

Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy lesser volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be more compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>
solution:
* Gas A and C have same value of ‘b’ but different value of a so gas having higher value of a have more force of attraction so molecules will be more closer hence occupy less volume.
* Gas B and D have same value of ‘a’ but different value of b so gas having lesser value of b will be more compressible.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Given, two closed bulbs of equal volumeV containing ideal gas initially of pressure pi  and  temperature T1, which are connected by a narrow tube of negligible volume.

To find the final pressure Pf, when one raised to T2:
Number of moles of gas does not change,
  nTi=nTf
P i V RT 1 + P i V RT 1 = P f V RT 1 + P f V RT 2
2 P i T 1 = P f T 1 + P f T 2 
2PiT1= Pf(T1 + T2)T1 × T2Pf = 2Pi (T2T1+ T2)

subject: Chemistry
solution: London dispersion Force (LDF), also known as london forces, or instantaneous dipole-induced dipole forces, is the weakest Vander-waal's force which arises in non-polar molecules due to temporary rearrangement of electrons.
